content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Shelba Powell 
City Clerk 
333 West Ocean 
Long Beach, CA 
Blvd. 
90802 
Dear Ms. Powell: 
November 26, 1985 
Re: 
Our File No. A-85-241 
I apologize for the delay in responding to your letter of 
October 14, 1985, in which you asked whether payments made by a 
potential candidate for the Long Beach City Council are reportable 
pursuant to the Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 
81000-91015). 
You provided a copy of a mailing sent by Dr. Jim SerIes, 
which included a~letter discussing Dr. SerIes' potential candidacy 
for the city council, a card on which residents were asked to state 
whether they did or did not think Dr. SerIes should run for city 
council, and a sheet containing Dr. SerIes' picture along with a 
list of his associations, activities, awards, education and community 
involvement. 
The top of this sheet contains the words "20 years of 
community service to the people of Long Beach." 
It is our conclusion that the mailing meets the definition of 
an "expenditure" as set forth in Government Code Section 82025 and 
FPPC regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225 (copy enclosed). 
Therefore, payments for the mailing should be reported. 
This con-
clusion is based on the following Commission interpretation. 
Payments made by an individual or entity (other than a person 
who meets the definition of "candidate" contained in Government 
Code Section 82007, or a "committee" as defined in Section 82013) 
in connection with activities which are strictly exploratory in 
nature, such as taking a poll of the voters to determine what 
issues are considered most important, or to determine the degree 
of name recognition an individual may have or the percentage of 
voters who might vote for a particular individual, generally are 
not considered to be "expenditures." 
However, if the poll or 
survey lists the potential candidate's qualifications or implies 
that the person is qualified for a particular office, or lists 
names of voters who are supporting or would vote for the person 
as a candidate, payments made in connection with the poll or 
survey are deemed to be "expenditures." 
The information sheet 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • (916) 322~5660 


Shelba Powell 
November 26, 1985 
Page Two 
containing Dr. SerIes' activities, awards, education and community 
involvement is a list of his qualifications. 
Therefore, payments 
for the mailing must be disclosed. 
I would also like to point out that if a person whose explora-
tory activities are not reportable for the reasons listed above 
ultimately decides to become a candidate, all payments in connec-
tion with the activities must be disclosed. 
If the person does not 
become a candidate, the payments are not reportable. 
Your second question asked when the expenditure should be 
reported by Dr. SerIes. 
Although Dr. SerIes has stated that he 
paid for the mailing after July 1, 1985, your letter indicates 
that the mailing may have been sent prior to July 1. 
Government 
Code Section 82025 states that an expenditure is made "on the 
date payment is made or on the date consideration, if any, is 
received, whichever is earlier." 
If Dr. SerIes mailed the 
materials prior to July 1, 1985, consideration in the form of 
printing and postage was received prior to July 1. 
Therefore, 
Dr. SerIes would have been required to file a campaign disclosure 
statement no later than July 31, 1985, covering the period 
January 1, 1985 through June 30, 1985. 
I hope the foregoing has been helpful. 
Please let me know 
if I can be of assistance in the future. 
Sincerely, 
{! MtL 
~<J 
tv~{l HJ..} 
Carla Wardlow 
Political Reform Consultant 
Enclosure 


333 WEST OCEAN 
October 14, 1985 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Technical Assistance & Analysis Division 
The Travelers Business Center 
428 J Street, Suite 800 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Gentlemen: 
Transmitted herewith are copies of a "mass mailer" sent to voters 
by a potential City Council candidate on or about July 1, 1985, 
along with a copy of a letter sent by this office to the candidate 
regarding our opinion that filing of a Campaign Statement relating 
to this expenditure is necessary. 
In a telephone conversation with the candidate on October 10, 1985, 
he indicated that he personally contacted the Los Angeles office of 
the FPPC prior to preparation of the mailer. 
He was informed that 
because it did not request contributions or votes and was not paid 
for by a committee, it was not to be considered "campaign related." 
Therefore, he would not have to report this expenditure on any 
Campaign Statement. 
(He also indicated that he paid for the mailer 
after July 1, 1985.) 
Conversely, in a conversation with your Los Angeles office on 
September 26, 1985, a member of our staff was informed that this 
type of mailer is considered "campaign-related" and therefore would 
require disclosure on a Campaign Statement. 
In view of the foregoing, it is respectfully requested that the 
Commission provide a written opinion for the following: 
1. 
Should the mailer be considered "campaign related?" 
2. 
Assuming that the mailer is "campaign related," if the candidate 
sent it prior to July 1, 1985 but paid for it after July 1, 1985, 
what reporting period dates should be used and when would the 
statement be due in this office? 
Thank you for your attention to this matter. 
SHELBA POWELL 
City Clerk 
Enclosures 
cc: 
Dr. James SerIes 


CITY CLERK 
333 WEST OCEAN BOULEVARD • 
LONG BEACH, CALIFORNIA 90802 
• 
(213) 590-6101 
Dr. James C. Serles 
171 Rivo Alto Canal 
Long Beach, Ca. 
90803 
Dear Dr. Serles: 
September 27, 1985 
It has come to my at~ention that you recently made a "mass 
mailing" (200 or more identical or nearly identical pieces 
sent within a calendar month) regarding your possible candidacy 
for City Council in the Third District in the April, 1986 
Primary Nominating Election. 
In accordance with campaign disclosure provisions of the Political 
Reform Act, "an individual who receives a contribution or makes 
an expenditure for the purpose of winning nomination or election 
to any elective office, whether or not the candidacy has been 
declared or the office sought is known must file campaign 
statements." 
Therefore, if you have had any contribution or expenditure 
activity since your last statement filed (6/10/82-12/31/82) 
you are required to file semi-annual campaign statements with the 
City Clerk. 
Semi-annual statements are due on July 31, 1985 and January 31, 
1986, with closing dates of June 30 and December 31, respectively. 
The "period covered" by the statement begins on-the day after the 
closing date of the most recent campaign statement filed (12/31/82) 
and ends on the closing date of the statement in question. 
Obviously, if you had any activity prior to June 30, 1985, you 
are late in filing your campaign statement. 
Hence, if you 
incurred expenses for your "mass mailing" prior to June 30, 1985 
you should file a statement for the period January 1, 1983 through 
June 30, 1985, along with a letter explaining why the statement was 
late, no later than October 10, 1985, with the City Clerk. 
If the expenses were incurred after July 1, 1985, your statement 
is not late, but you will have to file a statement for the period 
January 1, 1983 through December 31, 1985 by January 31, 1986. 
I am enclosing a copy of your most recent campaign statement 
filed alqng with a blank Form 490 (Consolidated Campaign Statement) 
for you to file at the appropriate time. 
If you should have any questions regarding campaign statements, 
please call Steve Ehren at (213) 590-6136. 
S~nce;rely) ,~ 
/ 
\/ ~/ ~ ;' / 
0' 
/'-sne{b't4D~~Ti--;-
attachments 


.. , 
Dear Neighbor, 
JIM SEKLES 
Doctor of Dental Surgery 
1 71 Kivo Alto Canal 
Long Beach, California 90803 
(213) 433,8187 
Three years ago, I ran for our City Council and I lost. 
I entered that election because I was convinced that our community 
needed someone who would give new leadership in providing a strong and 
positive direction within our city government. My interest in serving on our 
City Council stemmed from a desire to make Long Beach the kind of City 
where all of us want to reside and work. 
Even though I nearly won, by receiving 49% of the vote, 
have had to very 
seriously consider whether I would become a Council candidate in 1985. 
Many friends and neighbors have asked me to run again for our Third 
District Council seat. However, I have personally decided I will not mqke that 
decision until I have completed a district-wide study. 
During the next few months, I wiIl be walking throughout our Third 
District. I will be listening and asking you, and other local residents, what 
you feel are the important issues affecting our neighborhoods and City. 
From walking door-to-door and talking with you, I hope not only to find a 
sense of direction in resolving my own plans, but more importantly to learn 
and then share your ideas with other community leaders who share our 
positive goals for Long Beach. 
We have all seen our City changing ... a lot for the good ... and a lot for 
the bad. How we meet these extreme differences will, I believe, depend on 
how much each of us is willing to give to that extra effort called community 
pride and involvement. 
Please fill out the enclosed information card. When I have reached a final 
decision on whether or not to run for our City Council, I will let you know. I 
sincerely look forward to meeting and talking with you at your door in the 
next few weeks. 
Jim SerIes 


JIM SERLES 
"20 Years of Community Service 
to the People of Long Beach" 
Jim Series
